findings.title: BENCHMARK COMPETITIVENESS
findings.finding: BENCHMARK ranks 6 of 10 on risk-adjusted return; REGIME_SWITCHING leads by 0.10 Sharpe units.
evidence: Sharpe ranking (best → worst): REGIME_SWITCHING 0.62 · MOMENTUM_ROTATION 0.58 · EQUAL_WEIGHT 0.57 · RISK_PARITY 0.56 · VOL_TARGETING 0.54 · BENCHMARK 0.53 · MAX_SHARPE_ROLLING 0.52 · BLACK_LITTERMAN 0.48 · CLASSIC_60_40 0.48 · MIN_VARIANCE 0.44

CAGR ranking (best → worst): BENCHMARK 8.70% · REGIME_SWITCHING 7.70% · MOMENTUM_ROTATION 6.48% · EQUAL_WEIGHT 5.99% · CLASSIC_60_40 5.95% · MAX_SHARPE_ROLLING 5.62% · RISK_PARITY 5.41% · BLACK_LITTERMAN 5.39% · VOL_TARGETING 5.10% · MIN_VARIANCE 4.60%

Max-drawdown ranking (best → worst): VOL_TARGETING -18.97% · MOMENTUM_ROTATION -20.64% · MIN_VARIANCE -23.16% · RISK_PARITY -23.51% · MAX_SHARPE_ROLLING -23.57% · EQUAL_WEIGHT -28.76% · BLACK_LITTERMAN -28.76% · REGIME_SWITCHING -29.74% · CLASSIC_60_40 -35.27% · BENCHMARK -52.56%
BENCHMARK Sharpe rank: 6 of 10 (Sharpe 0.53); leader is REGIME_SWITCHING at 0.62 (margin 0.10).
findings.implication: A capital planning mandate that pays active management fees must clear this Sharpe margin before fees on a post-cost basis. REGIME_SWITCHING's 0.10-unit edge is the budget for active fees and execution slippage before the mandate falls behind a passive alternative.

findings.title: REGIME SHIFT EVIDENCE
findings.finding: Equity-IG correlation inverted in 2022; strategy dispersion widened materially.
evidence: Equity-IG rolling correlation: pre-2022 avg -0.05, post-2022 avg 0.57 (shift 0.62).

Strategies that DEGRADED post-2022: VOL_TARGETING 0.80 → -0.13 (Δ -0.94) · RISK_PARITY 0.75 → -0.12 (Δ -0.87) · MIN_VARIANCE 0.63 → -0.14 (Δ -0.76) · EQUAL_WEIGHT 0.71 → 0.03 (Δ -0.69) · BLACK_LITTERMAN 0.63 → 0.03 (Δ -0.60)
Post-2022 best-worst Sharpe gap: BENCHMARK 0.43 vs MIN_VARIANCE -0.14 (spread 0.57).
findings.implication: The 2022 break is the central finding of the project. Strategies that improved post-2022 are the ones that matter for a forward-looking mandate. Strategies that degraded performed well only when bonds diversified equity — that hedge is gone.

findings.title: TAIL RISK DIVERGENCE
findings.finding: Tail-risk dispersion is 0.42x between best (BENCHMARK) and worst (VOL_TARGETING) on CVaR 99% annual.
evidence: CVaR 99% annual ranking (best → worst): BENCHMARK -46.72% · MOMENTUM_ROTATION -34.10% · REGIME_SWITCHING -33.28% · CLASSIC_60_40 -31.17% · EQUAL_WEIGHT -31.06% · BLACK_LITTERMAN -31.06% · MAX_SHARPE_ROLLING -29.22% · RISK_PARITY -28.42% · MIN_VARIANCE -28.37% · VOL_TARGETING -19.84%
Ratio between highest and lowest CVaR 99%: 0.42
findings.implication: A Sharpe-only ranking hides material tail-risk differences. Two strategies with nearly identical Sharpe can have CVaR 99% diverging by 50%+ — the capital-planning loss budget depends on which one is chosen, not on the Sharpe-equivalent.

findings.title: NATURAL COMPLEMENTS
findings.finding: VOL_TARGETING and MOMENTUM_ROTATION are the lowest-correlation pair (r = 0.84); a naive 50/50 blend delivers Sharpe 0.80.
evidence: Lowest pairwise correlation pair (full period): VOL_TARGETING ↔ MOMENTUM_ROTATION at r = 0.84.
Individual Sharpe: VOL_TARGETING 0.79 · MOMENTUM_ROTATION 0.77.
Simulated 50/50 blend Sharpe: 0.80 (Δ vs best individual: 0.01).
Simulated 50/50 blend max drawdown: -19.38%.
findings.implication: Diversification benefit is largest where correlation is lowest. The 50/50 blend is the simplest demonstration of the free lunch — same realised data, better risk-adjusted return than either component on its own.

findings.title: EFFICIENT FRONTIER SHIFT
findings.finding: Deferred — tangency weights not present in payload.
findings.implication: Finding could not be computed from the current payload. Re-stage once the missing inputs land.

findings.title: DIVERSIFICATION BENEFIT
findings.finding: Equal-weight blend of the active strategies delivers Sharpe 0.72 vs benchmark 0.65 (Δ 0.06).
evidence: Equal-weight blend: Sharpe 0.72, max DD -25.11%, CAGR 5.50%.
BENCHMARK: Sharpe 0.65, max DD -52.56%, CAGR 9.03%.
Sharpe improvement over benchmark: 0.06.
Max-drawdown reduction vs benchmark: -27.45% (positive = blend is shallower).
findings.implication: Even naïve 1/N diversification across the active strategies improves on the passive benchmark. The marginal Sharpe is the budget for cost + complexity of a multi-strategy implementation; the drawdown reduction is the principal-protection benefit.

findings.title: MOMENTUM VS MEAN REVERSION
findings.finding: MOMENTUM_ROTATION + MIN_VARIANCE 50/50 blend delivers Sharpe 0.73 at correlation 0.90.
evidence: Momentum-driven: MOMENTUM_ROTATION (Sharpe 0.77).
Mean-reverting: MIN_VARIANCE — covariance-driven with no trend signal (Sharpe 0.69).
Pairwise correlation: 0.90.
50/50 blend Sharpe 0.73 · max DD -21.89% (vs MOMENTUM_ROTATION solo 0.77 · vs MIN_VARIANCE solo 0.69).
findings.implication: The momentum / mean-reversion axis is the most natural two-factor decomposition of the universe. A blend of the two delivers more Sharpe than either alone when their pairwise correlation is materially below 1 — the standard diversification math holds.

findings.title: CRISIS PERFORMANCE
findings.finding: 0 strategies clear BENCHMARK in every named crisis window.
evidence: GFC_2008-2009: BEST MOMENTUM_ROTATION (-9.48%) · WORST BENCHMARK (-38.61%) · BENCHMARK -38.61%.
EU_Debt_2011: BEST VOL_TARGETING (3.12%) · WORST MOMENTUM_ROTATION (-21.78%) · BENCHMARK -14.53%.
COVID_Crash_2020: BEST VOL_TARGETING (-27.84%) · WORST BENCHMARK (-73.53%) · BENCHMARK -73.53%.
COVID_Recovery: BEST BENCHMARK (41.86%) · WORST MIN_VARIANCE (13.68%) · BENCHMARK 41.86%.
Rate_Shock_2022: BEST MIN_VARIANCE (-14.31%) · WORST BENCHMARK (-19.44%) · BENCHMARK -19.44%.
No strategy beat BENCHMARK in every crisis window.
findings.implication: Crisis performance is the realised tail-risk evidence alongside the parametric CVaR. Strategies that beat the benchmark in all three windows have demonstrated stress resilience across distinct shocks (credit, liquidity, rates) — the strongest qualifier for a capital-planning allocation.

findings.title: FACTOR EXPOSURE
findings.finding: Market (MKT-RF) is the dominant variance driver; beta dispersion BENCHMARK 0.99 vs RISK_PARITY 0.38.
evidence: Average absolute beta dominant factor: MKT-RF (mean |β| = 0.53).
Highest market beta: BENCHMARK (β = 0.99).
Lowest market beta: RISK_PARITY (β = 0.38).
No strategy carries a materially negative MOM beta — momentum tilts in this cohort are all flat-to-positive.
findings.implication: Factor exposure dispersion across the cohort is the structural reason the strategies have low pairwise correlation. A planning mandate should target factor balance (not just notional dollar balance) when blending two or more strategies.

findings.title: MACRO CONTEXT ALIGNMENT
findings.finding: Current digest favours regime-aware and low-beta tilts; high-beta passives carry the most macro exposure.
evidence:
Digest summary: I'll start by running 5 parallel searches across the key macro categories to gather the latest signals from the past seven days.

Now I have sufficient sourced data to construct the digest. Let me fetch the FOMC minutes released May 20 and the CNBC Treasury yields article for additional depth on the…
Regime implication:
No key signals in current digest.
Most aligned (heuristic): REGIME_SWITCHING, RISK_PARITY, MIN_VARIANCE.
Most exposed to current risks: BENCHMARK, MOMENTUM_ROTATION.
findings.implication: Macro context is the bridge between the historical backtest and the forward-looking mandate. A strategy ranked first on full-period Sharpe is not necessarily the right pick for the next 12 months — the macro layer is what determines which historical edge is still alive.

findings.title: SURPRISES
findings.finding: No surprises in this run — every finding aligns with conventional portfolio-theory direction.
evidence: All ten preceding findings carry surprise=False. The ranking, regime split, tail-risk dispersion, diversification benefit, factor exposure and macro alignment all behave directionally as expected.
findings.implication: A 'no surprises' run is a positive signal — the underlying data is consistent with the academic literature this project rests on (Markowitz, Sharpe, Carhart, López de Prado). It does NOT mean every finding is HIGH-strength; magnitudes still vary.
